questions.text: When are the most babies born?
questions.narrative: Every hospital in the world gets busier on the same days. Not randomly — rhythmically. Something about the calendar pulls life toward certain weeks.
questions.revealCorrect: September. Nine months back from the busiest maternity wards sits New Year's Eve.
questions.revealWrong: September — not {guessedMonth}. Nine months back from the busiest maternity wards sits New Year's Eve.

questions.text: When are the most lives conceived?
questions.narrative: You got September. Now count back nine months. Most lives don't begin when we think they do — they begin in the middle of winter, when the world outside goes quiet.
questions.revealCorrect: December. The coldest month consistently produces the most conceptions. Nine months later, September fills up again.
questions.revealWrong: December — not {guessedMonth}. The coldest month consistently produces the most conceptions. Nine months later, September fills up again.

questions.text: When is the Earth at its hottest?
questions.narrative: You reached for summer. There's a reason — most of the planet's landmass sits in the northern hemisphere. When one half heats up, the global average follows.
questions.revealCorrect: July. The northern hemisphere tilts toward the sun and takes the rest of the planet's average with it.
questions.revealWrong: July — not {guessedMonth}. The northern hemisphere tilts toward the sun and takes the rest of the planet's average with it.

questions.text: When do the most people fall ill?
questions.narrative: You went for winter. Cold air, closed windows, shared rooms. Every year the same conditions line up, and every year the same thing happens.
questions.revealCorrect: January. The holidays end, everyone goes home, and the wave begins. It's been this way as long as we've tracked it.
questions.revealWrong: January — not {guessedMonth}. The holidays end, everyone goes home, and the wave begins. It's been this way as long as we've tracked it.
questions.source: CDC FluView / WHO seasonal illness data

questions.text: When does the Earth break the most?
questions.narrative: You chose autumn. The Atlantic hurricane season peaks, monsoons surge across Asia, and wildfire conditions hit their worst — all in the same narrow window.
questions.revealCorrect: September. More natural disasters cluster in this month than any other. The same weeks that produce the most births also produce the most destruction.
questions.revealWrong: September — not {guessedMonth}. More natural disasters cluster in this month than any other. The same weeks that produce the most births also produce the most destruction.

questions.text: When do the most flowering species bloom?
questions.narrative: You picked spring. Across the northern hemisphere, the same species flower within the same two-week window every year — a schedule older than any human calendar.
questions.revealCorrect: May. Peak bloom, globally averaged. The timing hasn't shifted much in centuries. The plants don't need reminding.
questions.revealWrong: May — not {guessedMonth}. Peak bloom, globally averaged. The timing hasn't shifted much in centuries. The plants don't need reminding.

questions.text: When do the most people die?
questions.narrative: Life has a rhythm. So does its end. Mortality data shows a consistent annual spike — not random, not scattered, but concentrated.
questions.revealCorrect: January. The same month that holds the most illness holds the most loss. The pattern repeats every year.
questions.revealWrong: January — not {guessedMonth}. The same month that holds the most illness holds the most loss. The pattern repeats every year.

questions.text: When do the most birds take flight?
questions.narrative: Billions of birds cross hemispheres every year on the same schedule. They don't check the weather — they respond to daylight length, a signal that hasn't changed in millennia.
questions.revealCorrect: May. The northern hemisphere warms, and the migration wave moves north. September reverses it.
questions.revealWrong: May — not {guessedMonth}. The northern hemisphere warms, and the migration wave moves north. September reverses it.

questions.text: When do sharks bite the most?
questions.narrative: Warmer water pushes baitfish closer to shore. Swimmers follow the heat. Sharks follow the fish. Every summer the same overlap happens.
questions.revealCorrect: July. More people in the water, more sharks near the coast. The peak isn't about aggression — it's about proximity.
questions.revealWrong: July — not {guessedMonth}. More people in the water, more sharks near the coast. The peak isn't about aggression — it's about proximity.
questions.source: Florida Museum of Natural History — International Shark Attack File

questions.text: When do most people pop the question?
questions.narrative: Proposals cluster. Not evenly across the year, but heavily in one month — roughly one in five happen in the same few weeks.
questions.revealCorrect: December. Holidays, family, the end of a year. People choose to begin something when everything else feels like it's wrapping up.
questions.revealWrong: December — not {guessedMonth}. Holidays, family, the end of a year. People choose to begin something when everything else feels like it's wrapping up.

questions.text: When does the world buy the most?
questions.narrative: Two of the largest economies on Earth have their biggest shopping events in the same month. Different cultures, different triggers, same timing.
questions.revealCorrect: November. Cyber Monday in the US. Singles' Day in China — the largest shopping event in human history, every November 11th.
questions.revealWrong: November — not {guessedMonth}. Cyber Monday in the US. Singles' Day in China — the largest shopping event in human history, every November 11th.
questions.source: Adobe Digital Economy Index / Alibaba Singles' Day data

questions.text: When do the most marriages end?
questions.narrative: Divorce filings follow a pattern. People decide during the holidays, hold on through January, and then act. There's a second spike when summer ends.
questions.revealCorrect: March. The first business weeks after winter. August is the other peak — school starts, summer's over, and there's nothing left to wait for.
questions.revealWrong: March — not {guessedMonth}. The first business weeks after winter. August is the other peak — school starts, summer's over, and there's nothing left to wait for.

questions.text: When does the world get the most light?
questions.narrative: You've seen births, heat, illness, migration, spending, loss. Every one had a peak. Every one had a month. One more question — and this one's different.
questions.revealCorrect: There isn't one. Averaged across both hemispheres, every day gets the same amount of sunlight.
questions.revealWrong: There isn't one. Averaged across both hemispheres, every day gets the same amount of sunlight.
